title: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities
section: Clinical Syndromes Health Care Associated Infections

items.subtopic: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities | ORGANIZATION, RESPONSIBILITIES, AND OVERSIGHT OF INFECTION PREVENTION AND CONTROL PROGRAMS
items.title: Importance of Infection Prevention and Control Programs in Healthcare Settings
items.content: Infection prevention and control programs are essential for managing nosocomial infections. These programs involve a team led by experienced infection preventionists, often including health care epidemiologists with doctoral-level training. They oversee activities such as surveillance, isolation practices, device-associated infection reduction, outbreak investigation, and collaboration on occupational health issues.
keyPoints: Health care-associated infections affect at least 3% of hospitalized patients at any given time.
Infection prevention programs include reporting to the National Healthcare Safety Network managed by CDC.
Reporting is required by U.S. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, affecting reimbursement.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities | ORGANIZATION, RESPONSIBILITIES, AND OVERSIGHT OF INFECTION PREVENTION AND CONTROL PROGRAMS

items.subtopic: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities | ORGANIZATION, RESPONSIBILITIES, AND OVERSIGHT OF INFECTION PREVENTION AND CONTROL PROGRAMS
items.title: Impact of Nosocomial Infection Reporting on Healthcare Reimbursement
items.content: The mandatory reporting of nosocomial infection data to the National Healthcare Safety Network impacts healthcare facilities' financial reimbursement. This system, managed by the CDC and required by U.S. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, ties reimbursement to the prevention of preventable infections.
keyPoints: Reporting to the National Healthcare Safety Network is mandatory.
Managed by the CDC.
U.S. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services requires reporting, affecting facility reimbursement.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities | ORGANIZATION, RESPONSIBILITIES, AND OVERSIGHT OF INFECTION PREVENTION AND CONTROL PROGRAMS

items.subtopic: SURVEILLANCE
items.title: Importance and Methods of Surveillance in Health Care-Associated Infections (HAIs)
items.content: Surveillance for HAIs involves monitoring outcomes, processes, and related measures to detect and prevent infections. Key outcome examples include surgical site infections and hospital-onset Clostridioides difficile infections. Process measures focus on compliance with evidence-based practices like hand hygiene and central line care. Health care personnel influenza immunization rates are tracked as a related measure.
keyPoints: Outcomes: Surgical site infections, hospital-onset Clostridioides difficile infections
Processes: Hand hygiene, central line insertion care, maintenance of indwelling devices
Related measures: Health care personnel influenza immunization rates
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · SURVEILLANCE

items.subtopic: SURVEILLANCE
items.title: Challenges and Advances in Electronic Surveillance Systems for HAIs
items.content: Laboratory-based surveillance is the primary method used by U.S. hospitals, supplemented with clinical reviews. Automated systems have improved efficiency but face challenges like reliability and standardization. Natural language processing models are expected to enhance these systems further.
keyPoints: Primary methodology: Laboratory-based surveillance
Supplemental methods: Clinical reviews by infection preventionists
Challenges: Reliability, interfacility standardization, need for human input
Advancements: Automated systems, natural language processing models
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · SURVEILLANCE

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGIC BASIS AND GENERAL MEASURES FOR PREVENTION AND CONTROL | COLONIZATION
items.title: Timing and Risk Factors for Nosocomial Infections
items.content: Nosocomial infections are those occurring after the first 48–72 hours of admission, distinguishing them from community-acquired or transferred infections. Key patient factors increasing susceptibility include invasive devices, immune deficiencies, renal insufficiency, diabetes, and other comorbidities.
keyPoints: Infections arising after 48-72 hours are considered nosocomial.
Risk factors: invasive devices, immune deficiency (congenital or acquired), renal insufficiency, diabetes, major comorbidities.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · EPIDEMIOLOGIC BASIS AND GENERAL MEASURES FOR PREVENTION AND CONTROL | COLONIZATION

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGIC BASIS AND GENERAL MEASURES FOR PREVENTION AND CONTROL | COLONIZATION
items.title: Colonization and Transmission of Multidrug-Resistant Organisms
items.content: Patients colonized with multidrug-resistant organisms, especially after antibiotic exposure, become reservoirs for transmission. Colonization pressure in a ward is a risk factor for spreading resistant organisms. Immunocompromised patients or those with breached immune barriers are at higher infection risk.
keyPoints: Altered microbiota from antibiotics increases susceptibility to colonization.
Colonized patients serve as reservoirs for multidrug-resistant organism transmission.
Colonization pressure is a risk factor for spreading resistant organisms.
Immunocompromised patients or those with breached barriers are at higher infection risk.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · EPIDEMIOLOGIC BASIS AND GENERAL MEASURES FOR PREVENTION AND CONTROL | COLONIZATION

items.subtopic: OUTBREAK INVESTIGATION AND RESPONSE | NOSOCOMIAL AND DEVICE-RELATED INFECTIONS | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS
items.title: Approach to Nosocomial Outbreak Investigation and Response
items.content: Outbreaks in healthcare settings are identified through surveillance or notifications. Molecular typing, especially whole genome sequencing for bacteria, is crucial for confirming relatedness of isolates and identifying resistance genes. The investigation process includes defining cases, reviewing records, conducting epidemiology, hypothesizing sources, implementing control measures, and maintaining communication.
keyPoints: Whole genome sequencing is the gold standard for determining clonality in bacterial outbreaks.
Outbreak response involves case definition, finding cases, descriptive epidemiology, hypothesis development, control measures, and surveillance.
Communication with patients, staff, facility leaders, and public health authorities is essential.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · OUTBREAK INVESTIGATION AND RESPONSE | NOSOCOMIAL AND DEVICE-RELATED INFECTIONS | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: OUTBREAK INVESTIGATION AND RESPONSE | NOSOCOMIAL AND DEVICE-RELATED INFECTIONS | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS
items.title: Prevention and Management of Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infections (CAUTIs)
items.content: CAUTIs are more common in critically ill, older patients, and females. Prevention focuses on sterile insertion techniques, maintaining closed drainage systems, avoiding unnecessary catheter use, and daily reassessment of catheter necessity. Asymptomatic bacteriuria should not be treated except in pregnant women to prevent inappropriate antimicrobial use.
keyPoints: Sterile and atraumatic catheter insertion and maintenance of a closed system are key prevention strategies.
Avoid unnecessary placement and prolonged use of indwelling urinary catheters.
Asymptomatic bacteriuria typically does not require treatment except in pregnant patients.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · OUTBREAK INVESTIGATION AND RESPONSE | NOSOCOMIAL AND DEVICE-RELATED INFECTIONS | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: HEALTH CARE-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED BLOODSTREAM INFECTIONS
items.title: Prevention Strategies for Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
items.content: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) is a significant subset of nosocomial pneumonia affecting up to 10% of ventilated patients, potentially prolonging ICU stays or causing fatalities. Preventive measures include daily reevaluation of the necessity for mechanical ventilation and minimizing invasive device use.
keyPoints: Ventilator-associated pneumonia occurs in up to 10% of ventilated patients.
VAP can extend ICU stay and be fatal.
Prevention includes limiting use of invasive devices and daily reassessment of central line necessity.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · HEALTH CARE-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED BLOODSTREAM INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: HEALTH CARE-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED BLOODSTREAM INFECTIONS
items.title: Impact and Prevention of Catheter-Associated Bloodstream Infections
items.content: Catheter-associated bloodstream infections significantly increase the risk of in-hospital death, tripling it. Preventive measures include aseptic insertion techniques using evidence-based checklists and daily full-body cleansing with 2% chlorhexidine gluconate in ICUs.
keyPoints: Catheter-associated bloodstream infections triple the risk of in-hospital death.
Prevention involves aseptic insertion and maintenance techniques.
Daily cleansing with 2% chlorhexidine gluconate is standard for ICU patients.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · HEALTH CARE-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA | CATHETER-ASSOCIATED BLOODSTREAM INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION-BASED PRECAUTIONS
items.title: Understanding Droplet Precautions for Respiratory Infections
items.content: Droplet precautions are essential when managing patients with respiratory symptoms or confirmed infections, as these pathogens can spread via droplets and aerosols. Protective equipment includes gowns, gloves, eye protection, and masks or respirators.
keyPoints: Respiratory viruses spread by droplets (≥100 microns) and aerosols (<100 microns).
Transmission occurs through inhalation of particles emitted during sneezing, coughing, talking, and breathing.
Droplets travel short distances and remain suspended briefly.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · TRANSMISSION-BASED PRECAUTIONS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION-BASED PRECAUTIONS
items.title: Implementing Airborne Precautions for High-Risk Pathogens
items.content: Airborne precautions are critical for pathogens like measles, tuberculosis, varicella, and COVID-19 that can travel long distances via tiny particles. These precautions include the use of respirators and placement in a negative-pressure isolation room.
keyPoints: Airborne transmission involves particles ≤100 microns that remain suspended longer.
Negative-pressure rooms require 12 air changes per hour with direct exhaust or HEPA filtration.
Combination precautions may be needed for certain conditions (e.g., varicella and SARS-CoV-2).
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · TRANSMISSION-BASED PRECAUTIONS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMICS, EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES, AND AGENTS OF BIOTERRORISM
items.title: Impact of Global Ecosystem Changes on Emerging Infectious Diseases
items.content: Global ecosystem changes such as deforestation, climate change, and urbanization increase human-wildlife interactions. This proximity can lead to cross-species spillover of zoonotic diseases, contributing to the emergence of infectious threats like Ebola, Zika, SARS-CoV-2, and mpox.
keyPoints: Deforestation, climate change, and urbanization facilitate closer human-wildlife interactions.
These factors can lead to cross-species spillover of zoonotic diseases.
Examples include the outbreaks of Ebola in 2014, Zika in 2016, SARS-CoV-2, and mpox.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · EPIDEMICS, EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES, AND AGENTS OF BIOTERRORISM

items.subtopic: EPIDEMICS, EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES, AND AGENTS OF BIOTERRORISM
items.title: Bioterrorism Preparedness: Key Pathogens and Vaccination Strategies
items.content: Health care personnel must be aware of bioterrorism threats from agents like Bacillus anthracis and variola virus. Smallpox vaccination ended in 1971, but vaccines are available for outbreak scenarios. B. anthracis poses a high fatality risk if aerosolized, necessitating preparedness protocols.
keyPoints: Bacillus anthracis is a category A pathogen with high case fatality and mass casualty potential if aerosolized.
Variola virus (smallpox) vaccination ended in 1971; vaccines are available for emergency use.
Two licensed smallpox vaccines: ACAM2000 and modified vaccinia Ankara, also used for mpox prevention.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · EPIDEMICS, EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES, AND AGENTS OF BIOTERRORISM

items.subtopic: VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL
items.title: Integrated All-Hazard Approach for Pandemic Preparedness
items.content: An integrated all-hazard approach is essential to manage new and reemerging contagious respiratory illnesses. This includes implementing infection control measures, initiating diagnostic testing, and ensuring safe delivery of care. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the necessity of maintaining adequate supplies of personal protective equipment, testing materials, and therapeutics. Regular training for healthcare personnel on proper use of PPE is crucial to safely manage patients with contagious diseases.
keyPoints: Integrated all-hazard screening approach is critical for infection control and safe care delivery.
Maintaining par levels of personal protective equipment and essential supplies is vital.
Regular training on PPE usage ensures readiness for handling contagious illnesses.
Testing capabilities should be rapidly adaptable to ensure timely diagnosis and management.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL | HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA

items.subtopic: HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA
items.title: High Impact of H5N1 Avian Influenza on Human Health
items.content: Avian influenza A (H5 and H7 strains) poses a significant pandemic threat due to its high pathogenicity and potential for human adaptation. The H5N1 strain has resulted in ~900 known human infections globally since 2003, with a case fatality rate of ~50%. Despite its severity, sustained person-to-person transmission has not yet occurred. Prevention measures include contact and airborne precautions, eye protection, and antiviral prophylaxis for close contacts.
keyPoints: ~900 known human infections worldwide since 2003 due to H5N1 avian influenza.
Case fatality rate of ~50% for H5N1 avian influenza.
Prevention measures include contact and airborne precautions, eye protection, and antiviral prophylaxis with oseltamivir.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL | HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA

items.subtopic: MPOX
items.title: Post-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk mpox exposures
items.content: Mpox, a zoonotic virus, spreads through close contact with mucosal surfaces or nonintact skin. Health care-associated transmission is rare unless there's direct inoculation like needlestick injuries.
keyPoints: Global outbreak of Clade II in 2022 and rise of Clade I cases in central Africa.
Transmission via close contact, contaminated objects; respiratory transmission less established.
Post-exposure prophylaxis with JYNNEOS or ACAM2000 vaccines recommended for high-risk exposures.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · MPOX | VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER PREPAREDNESS | CLOSTRIDIOIDES DIFFICILE INFECTION

items.subtopic: CLOSTRIDIOIDES DIFFICILE INFECTION
items.title: Prevention and management of C. difficile infection in healthcare settings
items.content: C. difficile is the most frequent hospital-acquired infection, often caused by antibiotic use. Antimicrobial stewardship and sporicidal disinfection are key to prevention.
keyPoints: Most common cause of health care-associated diarrhea.
Antibiotic receipt, especially quinolones and clindamycin, is a major risk factor.
Contact isolation for patients with suspected or confirmed infection.
Handwashing with soap and water preferred over alcohol-based gel; sporicidal disinfectants like bleach are used.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · MPOX | VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER PREPAREDNESS | CLOSTRIDIOIDES DIFFICILE INFECTION

items.subtopic: NOROVIRUS | TUBERCULOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS
items.title: Why Norovirus Outbreaks Persist in Healthcare Facilities
items.content: Norovirus is a significant cause of healthcare-associated diarrhea due to its resistance to alcohol-based hand gels and standard hospital cleaners. Effective control requires specific measures such as soap and water handwashing and bleach cleaning.
keyPoints: Norovirus is poorly inactivated by alcohol-based hand gel and standard hospital cleaners.
Transmission can occur among patients and staff, leading to outbreaks.
Control measures include handwashing with soap and water and bleach cleaning.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · NOROVIRUS | TUBERCULOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: NOROVUS | TUBERCULOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS
items.title: How to Prevent Tuberculosis Transmission in Healthcare Settings
items.content: Preventing tuberculosis transmission involves early identification, isolation, and testing of patients with suspected or confirmed TB. Health care personnel must use N95 respirators or powered air-purifying respirators for protection.
keyPoints: Use airborne precautions for managing patients with infectious tuberculosis.
Healthcare personnel should wear fit-tested particulate respirators (N95) that filter 95% of airborne particles.
Preemployment screening for TB is recommended using an interferon-γ release assay due to its higher positive predictive value.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · NOROVIRUS | TUBERCULOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: CANDIDA INFECTIONS | THE HEALTH CARE BUILT ENVIRONMENT
items.title: Understanding Candida auris Transmission in Health Care Settings
items.content: Candida auris is a multidrug-resistant yeast that poses significant challenges for infection control due to its ability to survive on surfaces and resist some disinfectants. It spreads through exogenous transmission, often via shared medical equipment like glucometers and ultrasound machines.
keyPoints: First isolated in Japan in 2009; emerged as a global nosocomial pathogen.
Incidence increased steadily since the first U.S. case in 2015, with acceleration during COVID-19.
Survives on surfaces for extended periods, complicating disinfection efforts.
Transmission can occur through shared medical equipment; thorough disinfection is crucial.
Hand hygiene and contact precautions are essential when managing colonized or infected patients.
Resistant to some hospital disinfectants; requires specific disinfectants.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · CANDIDA INFECTIONS | THE HEALTH CARE BUILT ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: CANDIDA INFECTIONS | THE HEALTH CARE BUILT ENVIRONMENT
items.title: Preventing Candida Infections in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Immunosuppressed patients, those who have undergone surgery, or those with invasive devices are at higher risk for endogenous Candida infections. Antifungal prophylaxis can lead to non-albicans species like Candida glabrata becoming more prevalent.
keyPoints: Candida albicans is the most common but antifungal prophylaxis may select out non-albicans species such as C. glabrata.
Immunosuppressed patients, surgical patients, and those with invasive devices are at increased risk.
Health care facilities must adhere to guidelines and surveillance to reduce infection risks.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · CANDIDA INFECTIONS | THE HEALTH CARE BUILT ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: ENVIRONMENTAL CLEANING | WATER SAFETY
items.title: Importance of Disinfection and Adjunctive Methods in Reducing Nosocomial Pathogen Transmission
items.content: Regular cleaning with disinfectant cleaners is crucial for reducing communicable bacteria, viruses, and fungi on floors and frequently touched surfaces. Additionally, patient care equipment should be disinfected between patients to prevent transmission of multidrug-resistant pathogens. The use of sporicidal cleaners and ultraviolet C light as adjunctive methods can further inactivate resilient pathogens like C. difficile spores.
keyPoints: Disinfectant cleaning reduces communicable bacteria, viruses, and fungi.
Patient care equipment should be disinfected between patients to prevent transmission of multidrug-resistant pathogens.
Sporicidal cleaners and ultraviolet C light can inactivate tenacious pathogens like C. difficile spores.
Clinical trial data suggest that standard cleaning plus adjunctive ultraviolet C may reduce nosocomial pathogen transmission over cleaning alone.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · ENVIRONMENTAL CLEANING | WATER SAFETY

items.subtopic: ENVIRONMENTAL CLEANING | WATER SAFETY
items.title: Water Management in Health Care Facilities to Prevent Waterborne Infections
items.content: Health care facilities must implement water management plans due to the risk of waterborne infections from both wastewater and potable water sources. Contamination often occurs when organisms like Legionella pneumophila colonize biofilms within plumbing systems, especially under conditions of low disinfectant levels, warm temperatures, and stagnant water. These factors can lead to aerosol or droplet transmission, particularly affecting immunosuppressed patients.
keyPoints: Waterborne infections are a risk from both wastewater and potable water sources in health care facilities.
Hospitals must have a water management plan to reduce patient risk.
Contamination occurs when organisms colonize biofilms within plumbing systems.
Low disinfectant levels, warm temperatures, and water stagnation promote organism growth.
Immunosuppressed patients are most susceptible; pneumonia should be investigated for waterborne infections.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · ENVIRONMENTAL CLEANING | WATER SAFETY

items.question: What is the role of hand hygiene in preventing transmission of pathogens in health care facilities?
options: It is not necessary if environmental cleaning is performed.
Hand hygiene can be performed with soap and water or alcohol-based gel to interrupt transmission.
Only alcohol-based hand gel should be used for all patients.
It is only required for health care personnel, not visitors.
items.explanation: Hand hygiene using soap and water or alcohol-based gel is essential to interrupt pathogen transmission.

The proportion of patients in a ward who are colonized, increasing risk for spread.
The frequency at which environmental cleaning is performed.
The level of antibiotic resistance among hospital staff.
items.explanation: Colonization pressure refers to the proportion of patients colonized, affecting the spread of resistant organisms.

items.question: Which pathogen is considered a category A agent with high case fatality and potential for mass casualty if aerosolized?
items.explanation: The passage identifies Bacillus anthracis as a category A pathogen with high case fatality and potential for mass casualty if aerosolized.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · EPIDEMICS, EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES, AND AGENTS OF BIOTERRORISM

items.subtopic: VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL | HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA
items.question: Which of the following is a critical component in handling patients with contagious respiratory illnesses?
options: Maintaining par levels of personal protective equipment and testing supplies.
Relying solely on commercial multiplex PCR panels for strain identification.
Avoiding regular training of health care personnel on PPE usage.
Delaying the activation of hospital incident command systems.
items.explanation: Maintaining par levels of personal protective equipment and testing supplies is vital, as emphasized by lessons from the COVID-19 pandemic.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL | HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA

items.subtopic: VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL | HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA
items.question: What is required for strain identification of novel influenza viruses in hospitals?
options: Commercial multiplex PCR panels are sufficient.
Dedicated testing in public health laboratories from specific samples.
Only nasopharyngeal swabs without additional tests.
No special measures are needed beyond standard protocols.
items.explanation: Strain identification requires dedicated testing in public health laboratories using samples like nasopharyngeal and conjunctival swabs, as commercial panels are insufficient.
items.reference: Infections Acquired in Health Care Facilities · VIRAL RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS WITH PANDEMIC POTENTIAL | HIGHLY PATHOGENIC AVIAN INFLUENZA

items.question: Which measure is recommended for controlling norovirus outbreaks in healthcare facilities?
items.explanation: Norovirus is poorly inactivated by alcohol-based hand gel, so handwashing with soap and water is recommended.

items.explanation: Health care personnel should use fit-tested particulate respirators, known as N95 respirators, to manage patients with infectious tuberculosis.
